title: Combining Multiple Vision Tasks
concept: Real production systems rarely use just one vision technique — they chain multiple applications together into a pipeline, where one technique's output becomes the next technique's input.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which Phase 10 technique directly answers 'where is the text and what does it say' in a photographed menu?
exercises.explain: Yes! OCR is purpose-built for locating and reading text.

cards.title: One system, many chained techniques
cards.body: Real production computer vision systems are rarely a single technique working alone — they're usually pipelines, where the output of one specialized tool feeds directly into the input of the next.

cards.title: Vision Task Pipelines
cards.body: A pipeline chains multiple vision applications together in sequence: detect first, then track, then recognize, then flag — each stage narrowing down or enriching the information, with earlier stages' outputs becoming later stages' inputs.

cards.title: An assembly line of specialists
cards.body: Think of a factory assembly line where each worker has one specialized job: one worker locates the part (detection), the next worker follows it down the belt (tracking), a third worker inspects it closely (anomaly detection). No single worker does everything — the pipeline as a whole gets the job done.

cards.title: A complete traffic monitoring pipeline
cards.body: 1. Object detection finds all vehicles in each frame. 2. Multi-object tracking follows each vehicle across frames, assigning IDs. 3. OCR reads each tracked vehicle's license plate. 4. Depth estimation computes each vehicle's distance and speed. Each stage builds directly on the previous stage's output.

cards.title: Pipelines aren't just 'running everything at once'
cards.body: It's a myth that combining techniques means just running every model on every frame independently and hoping the results line up. A real pipeline carefully passes STRUCTURED output from one stage (like tracked bounding boxes) as the deliberate INPUT to the next stage (like OCR cropping just those boxes) — the stages are designed to connect.

cards.title: Isolated Techniques vs. a Connected Pipeline
cards.body: The difference a well-designed pipeline makes:

left.text:
Each model runs independently
Outputs don't feed into each other
Results may not line up meaningfully

right.text:
Each stage's output feeds the next
Structured, deliberate hand-offs
Builds a complete end-to-end system

exercises.prompt: What defines a well-designed vision task pipeline?

options.text: Each stage's structured output deliberately becomes the input to the next stage
Running every possible model on every frame with no connection between them
Using exactly one vision technique and never combining any others
Randomly selecting which technique runs on each frame

exercises.explain: Correct! Deliberate, structured hand-offs between stages define a real pipeline.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A traffic monitoring pipeline could use tracking output (bounding boxes for each vehicle) as the direct input for an OCR stage reading license plates.
exercises.explain: True. This is a classic example of one stage's structured output becoming the next stage's deliberate input.

exercises.sentence: In a pipeline, each stage's output deliberately becomes the next stage's ___.
exercises.explain: Structured hand-offs between stages are the defining feature of a real vision pipeline.

exercises.prompt: Why is an assembly line with specialized workers a fitting analogy for a chained vision pipeline?

options.text: Each specialized stage handles one specific sub-task, passing its work forward, just like an assembly line worker passes a part to the next station
Assembly lines have no relationship to how software pipelines are structured
Every assembly line worker performs every single task simultaneously

exercises.explain: Correct! Specialized, sequential stages are the shared structure between both.

exercises.prompt: An engineer builds a pipeline where a detector finds pedestrians, and separately, a completely unrelated model classifies unrelated stock photos, with no connection between the two model outputs. Is this a real 'vision pipeline' in the sense we've discussed?

options.text: No — a real pipeline requires structured hand-offs where one stage's output deliberately feeds the next stage's input; unrelated independent models don't qualify
Yes — any two models running in the same codebase automatically form a pipeline
Yes — pipelines are defined purely by using more than one model, regardless of connection
This scenario is impossible to evaluate without more information about frame rate

exercises.explain: Exactly! True pipelines require deliberate, structured connections between stages. Next: bridging from research techniques to real production deployment.
